subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 11. In this unit you will learn to speak the language of graphs – one of the most powerful ideas in all of mathematics. A graph turns a set of numbers or a real situation into a picture that you can read at a glance. When Amina looks at a distance-time graph of her bus journey from Hargeisa to the next town, she can see in one moment where the bus was moving fast, where it stopped, and where it slowed down. That is the magic of a graph: it lets your eyes do the thinking.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 8 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Plot and read coordinates accurately in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane.
Build a table of values for a linear function such as y = 2x + 1.
Draw a straight-line graph from its equation using a table of values.
Understand the equation y = mx + c and identify the gradient (m) and the y-intercept (c).
Find the gradient of a line as rise ÷ run and describe whether it is positive, negative, zero or steep.
Read the equation of a straight line drawn on a grid.
Interpret real-world graphs, especially distance-time graphs, and describe what each section means.
Turn a real-life situation into a linear equation and draw its graph.
Solve problems by reading values from a graph and by finding where two lines intersect.

concepts.title: The Coordinate Plane
concepts.explanation: Every graph is drawn on a coordinate plane – two number lines that cross at right angles. The horizontal line is the x-axis and the vertical line is the y-axis. The point where they meet, (0, 0), is called the origin. Any point on the plane is named by a pair of numbers (x, y): the first number tells you how far to go across (right if positive, left if negative), and the second tells you how far to go up or down (up if positive, down if negative). The two axes divide the plane into four regions called quadrants,…
concepts.example: To plot a point, start at the origin every time. For (−2, 4) you move 2 units left along the x-axis, then 4 units up, and mark a neat cross. To read a point already on the grid, do the reverse: drop a straight line down…

concepts.title: Functions and Tables of Values
concepts.explanation: A function is a rule that turns each input number into exactly one output number. We usually call the input x and the output y, and we write the rule as an equation, for example y = 2x + 1. This rule says: take the x-value, multiply it by 2, then add 1. Feed in x = 3 and the rule gives y = 2(3) + 1 = 7. Feed in x = 0 and it gives y = 1. Each input has one clear output – that is what makes it a function. To draw the graph of a function we first make a table of values. This is simply an organised list of x-values…
concepts.example: Read the bottom row: when x goes up by 1, y goes up by 3 every single time (−8, −5, −2, 1, 4). This constant difference is the fingerprint of a linear function – it is exactly why the graph will be a straight line. If…

concepts.title: The Equation y = mx + c
concepts.explanation: This is the central idea of the whole unit. Every straight-line graph can be written in the form y = mx + c, where m and c are numbers. These two letters carry all the information you need to draw or describe the line: m is the gradient – it tells you how steep the line is and which way it slopes.
concepts.example: c is the y-intercept – it tells you where the line crosses the y-axis, that is, the value of y when x = 0.

concepts.title: Understanding Gradient
concepts.explanation: The gradient measures how steep a line is. We calculate it as rise ÷ run, which means the change in y divided by the change in x as you move from one point on the line to another: gradient = (change in y) ÷ (change in x) = rise ÷ run
concepts.example: Imagine walking along the line from left to right. The run is how far you move across; the rise is how far you move up (or down) in that stretch. If a line rises 6 units while you run 3 units across, its gradient is 6 ÷…

concepts.title: Drawing a Linear Graph
concepts.explanation: Now we bring the ideas together and actually draw a line. There is more than one method, and it is worth knowing each so you can choose the quickest for a given problem. This is the most reliable method and works for any linear equation. To draw y = 2x − 1, first build a short table, then plot the points and join them with a ruled straight line.
concepts.example: Plot the points (0, −1), (1, 1), (2, 3) and (3, 5), then lay your ruler along them and draw a single straight line right across the grid, extending it a little past the end points. The points should all sit exactly on…

concepts.title: Reading the Equation of a Line
concepts.explanation: So far we have gone from equation to graph. Now we go the other way: given a line drawn on a grid, we find its equation. Because every straight line is y = mx + c, we just need to find c and m. First find c, the y-intercept, by reading where the line crosses the y-axis. Suppose a line crosses at (0, 1); then c = 1. Next find m, the gradient, by choosing two clear points where the line passes exactly through grid corners and working out rise ÷ run between them. Suppose the line also passes through (2, 5); then from…
concepts.example: Always choose points that sit neatly on grid intersections – reading the gradient between two 'nice' points is far more accurate than guessing from points that fall between the lines. And watch the direction: if the…

methods.title: How to draw a line from an equation
methods.example: Complete a table of values for y = 4x − 3 using x = −1, 0, 1, 2, 3.
steps: Choose three or more x-values, including 0.
Substitute each into the equation to find y, building a table.
Plot the points (x, y) on the grid.
Join them with a ruled straight line and label it with its equation.

methods.title: How to find the equation of a drawn line
methods.example: For y = −3x + 2, state the gradient and the y-intercept.
steps: Read c where the line crosses the y-axis.
Pick two clear points on grid corners.
Work out m = rise ÷ run between them (watch the sign).
Write y = mx + c and check with a third point.

methods.title: How to find where two lines cross
methods.example: Find the gradient of the line through (2, 1) and (6, 9).
steps: Draw both lines on the same grid, or set their equations equal.
Solve for x, then substitute back to find y.
The point (x, y) is where the two quantities are equal.

methods.title: Draw a line using intercept and gradient
methods.example: Describe how to draw y = 2x + 1 without a full table.
steps: Solution: The intercept is c = 1, so start at (0, 1).
The gradient is m = 2 = rise 2 over run 1, so move 1 right and 2 up to reach (1, 3), then again to (2, 5).
Rule a straight line through the points.
Check: substituting x = 2 gives y = 5, which matches.

methods.title: Read the equation of a drawn line
methods.example: A line crosses the y-axis at (0, −2) and also passes through (3, 4). Find its equation.
steps: Solution: The intercept gives c = −2.
The gradient from (0, −2) to (3, 4) is rise (4 − (−2)) = 6 over run (3 − 0) = 3, so m = 6 ÷ 3 = 2.
The equation is y = 2x − 2.

methods.title: Interpret a distance-time graph
methods.example: A distance-time graph rises steeply, then goes flat, then rises gently. Describe the journey.
steps: Solution: The steep rise means moving quickly away from the start.
The flat section means stopped (time passes but distance does not change).
The gentle rise means moving slowly away again.
The gradient of each section is the speed, so steeper always means faster.

workedExamples.title: Build a table of values
workedExamples.prompt: Complete a table of values for y = 4x − 3 using x = −1, 0, 1, 2, 3.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Substitute each x. When x = −1: 4(−1) − 3 = −4 − 3 = −7. When x = 0: −3. When x = 1: 4 − 3 = 1. When x = 2: 8 − 3 = 5. When x = 3: 12 − 3 = 9. So y = −7, −3, 1, 5, 9. The constant difference of 4 confirms it is linear.

workedExamples.title: Read m and c from an equation
workedExamples.prompt: For y = −3x + 2, state the gradient and the y-intercept.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Match to y = mx + c. The number with x is m = −3 (a negative gradient, so the line slopes downhill). The number on its own is c = 2, so the line crosses the y-axis at (0, 2).

workedExamples.title: Find a gradient from two points
workedExamples.prompt: Find the gradient of the line through (2, 1) and (6, 9).
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Change in y = 9 − 1 = 8. Change in x = 6 − 2 = 4. Gradient = rise ÷ run = 8 ÷ 4 = 2. The line slopes uphill because the gradient is positive.

workedExamples.title: Draw a line using intercept and gradient
workedExamples.prompt: Describe how to draw y = 2x + 1 without a full table.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The intercept is c = 1, so start at (0, 1). The gradient is m = 2 = rise 2 over run 1, so move 1 right and 2 up to reach (1, 3), then again to (2, 5). Rule a straight line through the points. Check: substituting x = 2 gives y = 5, which matches.

workedExamples.title: Read the equation of a drawn line
workedExamples.prompt: A line crosses the y-axis at (0, −2) and also passes through (3, 4). Find its equation.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The intercept gives c = −2. The gradient from (0, −2) to (3, 4) is rise (4 − (−2)) = 6 over run (3 − 0) = 3, so m = 6 ÷ 3 = 2. The equation is y = 2x − 2.

workedExamples.title: Interpret a distance-time graph
workedExamples.prompt: A distance-time graph rises steeply, then goes flat, then rises gently. Describe the journey.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The steep rise means moving quickly away from the start. The flat section means stopped (time passes but distance does not change). The gentle rise means moving slowly away again. The gradient of each section is the speed, so steeper always means faster.

workedExamples.title: Model a real situation
workedExamples.prompt: A plumber charges a 50-shilling call-out fee plus 40 shillings per hour. Write the equation and find the cost of a 3-hour job.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Fixed charge c = 50, rate m = 40, so C = 40h + 50. For h = 3: C = 40(3) + 50 = 120 + 50 = 170 shillings. On a graph this is the line's height above h = 3.

workedExamples.title: Find where two lines intersect
workedExamples.prompt: Find the intersection of y = x + 2 and y = −x + 6, and say what it means.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: At the crossing point the two y-values are equal, so x + 2 = −x + 6. Adding x to both sides: 2x + 2 = 6, so 2x = 4 and x = 2. Then y = 2 + 2 = 4. The lines cross at (2, 4) – the point where both rules give the same value.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Find the gradient of the line through (2, 3) and (6, 15).

workedExamples.title: Are y = 2x + 1 and y = 2x − 4 parallel? Explain
workedExamples.prompt: Are y = 2x + 1 and y = 2x − 4 parallel? Explain.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: For y = ½x + 1, find y when x = 6, and find x when y = 0.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Find where y = 3x − 2 and y = x + 4 intersect, using algebra.

activities.title: Human Coordinate Grid
activities.materials: an open floor or yard, chalk or tape, and a few people.
steps: Mark out an x-axis and a y-axis on the ground with chalk or tape, numbered from −4 to 4.
Take turns calling out coordinates like (3, −2) and have someone stand on the correct spot.
Check each other: did they go along first, then up or down?
Now call out a quadrant, and everyone in that quadrant hops. Discuss the sign rules for each quadrant.
Moving your whole body to a coordinate makes the 'along then up' rule stick.

activities.title: The Gradient Walk
activities.materials: a set of steps or a ramp, and a tape measure or ruler.
steps: Find a staircase or ramp. Measure how far it rises (up) and how far it runs (across) over a fixed stretch.
Work out the gradient as rise ÷ run.
Compare a gentle ramp with a steep staircase – which has the bigger gradient?
Sketch each as a straight-line graph and label which is steeper.
This shows that gradient is just a number for steepness – the same idea whether it is stairs or a graph.

activities.title: Distance-Time Story Game
steps: One player invents a short journey story, for example: 'I walk quickly to the masjid, pray, then stroll home.'
The other player sketches the distance-time graph for that story.
Swap roles and check each other's graphs: does a flat section really mean stopped?
Try trickier stories, such as running there but walking back, and compare the steepness of each part.

activities.title: Real Data Line Graph
activities.materials: graph paper, a ruler, and some real data you can collect.
steps: Collect data over time, such as the temperature each hour, or how far a toy car rolls each second.
Choose sensible scales so the data fills most of the page, then plot the points.
Decide whether the points lie roughly on a straight line, and if so, estimate its gradient.
Write one sentence describing what the graph tells you about the real situation.

activities.title: The Two-Plan Investigation (Family Task)
activities.materials: graph paper, a ruler, and a family member to discuss with.
steps: Invent two mobile-phone or savings plans, each with a starting amount and a rate (for example 30 + 2 per week, and 50 + 1 per week).
Write each as an equation in the form y = mx + c.
Draw both lines on one grid and find where they cross.
Explain to your family member which plan is better for a small amount and which is better for a large amount, using the crossing point.

activities.title: Equation Match-Up Game
steps: On one set of cards write equations like y = 2x + 1, y = −x + 3, y = ½x − 2.
On another set write the matching gradient and intercept, such as 'm = 2, c = 1'.
Shuffle both sets, place them face down, and play a memory-matching game.
Each time you turn over a pair, say aloud why they match before keeping them.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand gradient best? What does a flat section on a distance-time graph mean? When would finding where two lines cross be useful in real life? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: A rule where each input x gives exactly one output y

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: A function is a rule that turns each input number into exactly one output number. We usually call the input x and the output y, and we write the rule as an equation, for example y = 2x + 1. This rule says: take the x-value, multiply it by 2, then add 1. Feed in x = 3 and the rule gives y = 2(3) + 1 = 7. Feed in x = 0 and it gives y = 1. Each input has one clear output – that is what makes it a function.

rules.title: Key idea 3
rules.text: For an equation like y = 3x + 6 you can find where it crosses each axis. Setting x = 0 gives y = 6, so the line passes through (0, 6). Setting y = 0 gives 0 = 3x + 6, so x = −2, giving the point (−2, 0). Plot these two intercepts and rule a line through them. This is neat when the intercepts come out as tidy whole numbers.

rules.title: Key idea 4
rules.text: Always choose points that sit neatly on grid intersections – reading the gradient between two 'nice' points is far more accurate than guessing from points that fall between the lines. And watch the direction: if the line slopes downhill from left to right, the gradient must come out negative, so expect a minus sign. Halima checks her answer by picking a third point on the line and testing it in her equation; if it fits, she can be confident she has the right rule.

rules.title: Key idea 5
rules.text: This skill matters because real data usually arrives as a graph, not an equation. A scientist measuring how a plant grows, or a trader tracking prices, reads the gradient and intercept off the graph to find the underlying rule – and once they have the equation, they can predict values they never measured.

rules.title: Key idea 6
rules.text: Solution: The intercept is c = 1, so start at (0, 1). The gradient is m = 2 = rise 2 over run 1, so move 1 right and 2 up to reach (1, 3), then again to (2, 5). Rule a straight line through the points. Check: substituting x = 2 gives y = 5, which matches.

terms: A pair (x, y) fixing a point's position
Steepness of a line: rise ÷ run
Where a line crosses the y-axis (x = 0)
Where a line crosses the x-axis (y = 0)
A graph that is a straight line
A list of x-values and the y-values they give
The point where two lines cross
commonMistakes: Go along the x-axis first, then up
Gradient = rise ÷ run (change in y over x)
A downhill line has a negative gradient
Number with x = m; number alone = c

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf leaves Hargeisa by car. A distance-time graph shows the line rising steeply for 20 minutes, flat for 10 minutes, then rising gently for 30 minutes. Describe his journey and say in which part he travelled fastest.
realProblems.answer: 1) Steep rise = driving fast away from Hargeisa; flat = stopped; gentle rise = driving slowly. Fastest in the first (steepest) part.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Steep rise = driving fast away from Hargeisa; flat = stopped; gentle rise = driving slowly. Fastest in the first (steepest) part.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find where y = 3x − 2 and y = x + 4 intersect, using algebra.
A line passes through (−2, −7) and (1, 2). Find its gradient and equation in the form y = mx + c.
A taxi charges 5 shillings base plus 3 shillings per km. Write the equation, draw the graph for 0 to 5 km, and read off the cost of a 4 km trip.
Two lines have equations y = 4x and y = 2x + 6. At what point do they cross, and what does the crossing mean if they are two different savings plans?
Explain the key idea in Drawing a Linear Graph.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Now we bring the ideas together and actually draw a line. There is more than one method, and it is worth knowing each so you can choose the quickest for a given problem. This is the most reliable method and works for any linear equation. To draw y = 2x − 1, first build a short table, then plot the points and join them with a ruled straight line.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Reading the Equation of a Line.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: So far we have gone from equation to graph. Now we go the other way: given a line drawn on a grid, we find its equation. Because every straight line is y = mx + c, we just need to find c and m. First find c, the y-intercept, by reading where the line crosses the y-axis. Suppose a line crosses at (0, 1); then c = 1. Next find m, the gradient, by choosing two clear points where the line passes exactly through grid corners and working out rise ÷ run between them. Suppose the line also passes through (2, 5); then from…

selfAssessment: I can plot and read coordinates accurately in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane.
I can build a table of values for a linear function such as y = 2x + 1.
I can draw a straight-line graph from its equation using a table of values.
I can understand the equation y = mx + c and identify the gradient (m) and the y-intercept (c).
I can find the gradient of a line as rise ÷ run and describe whether it is positive, negative, zero or steep.
I can read the equation of a straight line drawn on a grid.
I can interpret real-world graphs, especially distance-time graphs, and describe what each section means.
I can turn a real-life situation into a linear equation and draw its graph.
